text: Nobody touches the shepherd's pie till I've been in there. Don't bust my crust, baby.
speaker: Keith Richards
context: The Rolling Stones guitarist explaining his strict on-the-road rule about his pre-show shepherd's pie, written in his autobiography 'Life'. Oddly specific and very funny rock-star demand.

source.title: Don't mess with Keith Richards' pre-show shepherd's pie ritual, or he might threaten to cut you up and bake you into one

text: If I'm playing and it's a Chinese driver, He'll turn around like, 'That reminds me of my country!' Or if it's a Japanese driver… or if it's an African. I'm playing the same flute, but every nationality of driver will turn around and tell me it reminds them of their country.
speaker: André 3000
context: The Outkast rapper, on Questlove's 'Questlove Supreme' podcast, describing how he'd play his flute in the back of Ubers and how drivers of every nationality told him it reminded them of their home country

source.title: How Did André 3000 Learn To Play Flute?

text: She was flamboyant. She had bright red lipstick, long red fingernails. She had high-heeled shoes, little floating plastic goldfish in the heels of them, short skirts, low-cut tops, and I just thought she was beautiful.
speaker: Dolly Parton
context: In a Guardian interview (quoted by HuffPost), Dolly Parton describes the local 'town tramp' whose flashy style inspired her own signature look as a child.

source.title: Dolly Parton Says Her Signature Style Was Inspired By The Actual 'Town Tramp'

text: No. I have no technical ability. And I know nothing about music.
speaker: Rick Rubin
context: Super-producer Rick Rubin (Def Jam co-founder; produced Johnny Cash, Beastie Boys, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele, Jay-Z) when Anderson Cooper asked on 60 Minutes whether he knew how to work a soundboard.

source.title: Rick Rubin: The 60 Minutes Interview (transcript) — interview by Anderson Cooper, CBS 60 Minutes

text: I have a credit on the back of the album that says, 'Nails by Dolly!'
speaker: Dolly Parton
context: Dolly Parton explaining that she composed and partly performed the percussive 'typewriter' rhythm of '9 to 5' by clacking her long acrylic fingernails together — and that the album '9 to 5 and Odd Jobs' literally credits 'Nails by Dolly.'

source.title: “I have a credit on the back of the album that says, ‘Nails by Dolly!’” Dolly Parton on the hit song she wrote without a

text: A genius is the one most like himself.
speaker: Thelonious Monk
context: One line from 'T. Monk's Advice' — a set of pointers the jazz pianist Thelonious Monk gave saxophonist Steve Lacy, who wrote them down while playing in Monk's band in 1960.

source.title: Thelonious Monk’s Advice — Howard Rees’ Jazz Workshops (transcribed by Steve Lacy, 1960)

text: My topic is the shift from 'architect' to 'gardener', where 'architect' stands for 'someone who carries a full picture of the work before it is made', to 'gardener' standing for 'someone who plants seeds and waits to see exactly what will come up'.
speaker: Brian Eno
context: Composer/producer Brian Eno (Roxy Music, ambient music pioneer, producer for Bowie, Talking Heads, U2) framing how he thinks about making music — not as an architect with the finished work in his head, but as a gardener who plants seeds

source.title: Composers as Gardeners — Brian Eno, Edge.org

text: Why am I crying on Oprah?
speaker: Pharrell Williams
context: On Oprah Winfrey's talk show 'Oprah Prime' (April 2014), Oprah surprised Pharrell by playing a montage of fan videos of people around the world dancing to his hit song 'Happy.'

source.title: Watch Pharrell Cry Over 'Happy' Inspiration During Interview with Oprah Winfrey

text: Y'all can't read this, can you?
speaker: Stevie Wonder
context: Presenting Song of the Year at the 2016 Grammy Awards (alongside Pentatonix), the blind musician held up the winner's envelope, which was written in Braille, and playfully teased his fellow presenters and the audience.

source.title: Stevie Wonder Charms GRAMMYs Audience With Braille Joke, Important Message

text: I can't possibly accept this award.
speaker: Adele
context: Accepting Album of the Year for '25' at the 2017 Grammy Awards, Adele stunned the room by opening her acceptance speech declaring she couldn't accept the award, then turning to praise Beyonce — whose 'Lemonade' she felt should have won.

source.title: GRAMMY Rewind: Adele Urges That Beyonce's 'Monumental' 'Lemonade' Should've Won Album Of The Year In 2017

text: It's not about how many times you get rejected ... it's about how many times you stand up and are brave and you keep on going.
speaker: Lady Gaga
context: Accepting the Oscar for Best Original Song ('Shallow' from 'A Star Is Born') at the 2019 Academy Awards, Lady Gaga delivered an emotional, encouraging acceptance speech about perseverance and not giving up on your dreams.

source.title: Lady Gaga's 2019 Oscars Acceptance Speech: "If You Have A Dream, Fight For It"

text: He built this entire empire based on what he thought would turn the world on. That was his superpower
speaker: Rob Thomas

source.title: Music — Rob Thomas Remembers Clive Davis: ‘Losing Clive Is the End of an Era’
